1:
On Matthew 8:1 — “When he was come down from the mountain, great multitudes followed him” — the witnesses read the verse as the hinge between the Sermon and the miracles that confirm it, and several find a deeper meaning in the descent.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene and the sequence. Jerome notes that “after the preaching and teaching is offered an occasion of working miracles, that by mighty works the preceding doctrine might be confirmed.” Ellicott lays out the differing order in Matthew, Mark, and Luke, concluding the juxtaposition of teaching and act “fixed itself on men’s minds.” Augustine harmonizes Luke’s placing of the leper elsewhere as “his manner… to recollect things omitted.” Clarke supplies a summary of the whole chapter’s miracles.

The fathers read the descent allegorically. Pseudo-Chrysostom: as Christ “taught as one having authority,” so he now shows the same power in works; and the leper, “bearing the burden of sin… for the sin of our souls is a leprosy,” could not climb the mount but meets the Lord who “came down from the height of heaven… that he might purge the leprousness of our sin.” Pseudo-Origen adds that on the mount the disciples alone were with him, to whom “it was given to know the secret things,” but coming down — stooping “to the infirmity… of the rest” — the crowds followed, “some for renown, most for his doctrine, some for cures.”

The Reformed Calvin marks the “striking display of divine power” in cleansing leprosy “by his word alone and a touch of his hand.” The Puritan Henry draws the devotional note: the people, astonished at his doctrine, “could not but desire to be better acquainted with him” — “they to whom Christ has manifested himself… should covet to know more,” following on to know the Lord. So the verse both closes the discourse and opens the gallery of works that seal its authority. Calvin adds that this leprosy was a disease which, by the Law, God reserved to himself to heal, so that its instant cleansing “by his word alone and a touch of his hand” was no common cure but a manifest token of divine power. Jerome had noted the same purpose: “that by mighty works following, the preceding doctrine might be confirmed” — the Sermon’s teaching and the chapter’s miracles being, in the witnesses’ view, two halves of one self-attestation of the King and his kingdom.
2:
On Matthew 8:2 — “there came a leper and worshipped him, saying, Lord, if thou wilt, thou canst make me clean” — the witnesses agree the leper’s words show a rare faith in Christ’s power joined to submission to his will, and several read leprosy as the type of sin.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the disease and the faith. Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott describe the dreadful, “loathsome” and contagious malady — “deemed incurable by any human means,” so that “among the Jews, God alone was applied to for its removal,” the cure “ever attributed to his sovereign power” (Numbers 12:10; 2 Chronicles 26:19). Robertson catches the precise faith: “the leper knew that Jesus had the power… his doubt was about his willingness” — “men more easily believe in miraculous power than in miraculous love” (Bruce) — a third-class condition, “a hopeful doubt.” Lightfoot draws out the rabbinic doctrine of leprosy as “the doctrine of sin,” no creature so unclean by a touch as man.

On the “worship,” the fathers and Reformed differ in emphasis. Chrysostom marks the leper’s reverence: he said not “if thou wilt ask of God” nor “cleanse me,” but “left all to him, thereby making him Lord and attributing to him the power over all.” Calvin weighs proskynein, concluding the outward gesture was a token of the reverence common among the Eastern peoples, not necessarily “divine worship,” though it confessed Christ’s extraordinary power. The Puritan Henry draws the pattern of prayer: “when we cannot be sure of God’s will, we may be sure of his wisdom and mercy”; we must “commend ourselves to his pity… humbly request it as a favour,” not demand it “as a debt.” Remigius reads the leper as the sinner who “falls down before Christ when he is confounded concerning his former sins,” confessing his disease and seeking the cure of penitence — adding that “the Church ought not to be reconciled to the same, but on the sentence of the priest.” Barnes and Clarke detail the disease’s horror — spreading scales, “highly contagious, so as to infect garments and houses” (Leviticus 13:47), and reckoned incurable — which makes the leper’s confident appeal to Christ’s mere will the more remarkable. Wesley notes that such, “though he might not mix with the people,” had heard the Lord “at a distance.”
3:
On Matthew 8:3 — “Jesus put forth his hand, and touched him, saying, I will; be thou clean. And immediately his leprosy was cleansed” — the witnesses marvel at the touch that did not defile, the sovereign word, and the instant cure.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the authority. Clarke: “the most sovereign authority is assumed… I will: there is here no supplication of any power superior to his own,” and “a single word is enough” — the leprosy cleansed “in a moment of time.” Barnes notes that to touch a leper “was regarded as making him ceremonially impure” (Leviticus 13:3), so the act “expressed the intention of Jesus to cure him.” Ellicott bids us “picture the process… the skin cleansed, the sores closed,” and adds Mark’s note that Jesus “had compassion on him.”

The fathers and Reformed dwell on the purity that conquered uncleanness. Chrysostom: he “put out his hand… to show that he was not subject to the Law, and that to the pure nothing is impure” — unlike Elisha, who kept his distance from Naaman, the Lord “heals and touches” as Lord, “his hand was not made unclean… but the leprous body was made clean.” Calvin draws the deeper truth: as Christ’s purity “repels all filth,” in touching the leper he contracted no defilement; so, taking our flesh, he “was united to one and the same body with ourselves… descended even to hell, and yet contracted no stain… but, retaining his innocence, took away all our impurities, and sprinkled us with his holiness.” Gill marks the threefold display — “the grace and goodness of Christ in touching this loathsome creature… his unspotted purity… and his mighty power in healing by a touch.” Lightfoot notes the wonder that, where leprosy was a creeping infection, the touch left the healer untouched — recalling that Miriam and Uzziah were smitten with leprosy where the priest who judged it was not. Ellicott bids us “venture to picture the process… the skin cleansed, the sores closed, the diseased whiteness giving way to the tints of health,” and adds from Mark that Jesus was “moved with compassion” — so that the touch was at once a pledge of power, a proof of purity that could not be defiled, and an act of tender mercy toward an outcast no one else would come near.
4:
On Matthew 8:4 — “See thou tell no man; but go thy way, shew thyself to the priest, and offer the gift that Moses commanded, for a testimony unto them” — the witnesses discuss the charge of silence, the sending to the priest, and the offering.

On the silence, the grammatical-historical commentators give several reasons. Clarke: had Christ “at this early period fully manifested himself as the Messiah, the people would have proclaimed him King,” stirring the rulers’ hatred and the governor’s jealousy. Wesley adds the aims of preventing the crowd from thronging him (Mark 1:45), fulfilling the prophecy that he would not be ostentatious (Isaiah 42:1), and avoiding being “made a king” (John 6:15). Barnes notes the charge held only until the man had made “proper representation to the priest,” whose sanction would attest the cure (Leviticus 14).

On the sending to the priest, Jerome reads Christ’s humility, that “he may seem to defer to the priests,” while Ellicott marks three ends: obedience to the Law (so showing he came “not to destroy the Law, but to fulfil”), the appointed “test of the reality… of the cleansing,” and the good of the man’s own soul, to “cherish his gratitude” rather than “waste it in many words.” Lightfoot recalls that, however “degenerate” the priesthood, Christ “would reserve to it its privileges.” Bede observes the typical sacrifices were not yet abolished, since Christ “had not yet offered his body for a holocaust.”

The Reformed Calvin judges the cleansed leper, who (by Mark) spread the report, “ought to be condemned for not obeying Christ’s injunction,” the “full time not yet being come” (John 7:6). Across the voices, the command joins humility, obedience to the Law, and a holy reticence that refused premature acclaim. Gill explains the charge was “that whilst he was on the road to Jerusalem… he would speak of it to no man, before he came to the priest,” lest the priests, “out of ill will to Christ,” refuse to pronounce him clean. Bede draws the typological note that this leper, “full of leprosy,” figures the whole human race — “all have sinned, and need the glory of God” — cleansed when the Saviour’s hand, the Word, is stretched out to touch our nature.
5:
On Matthew 8:5 — “when Jesus was entered into Capernaum, there came unto him a centurion, beseeching him” — the witnesses note the Gentile petitioner, the type he becomes, and the harmony with Luke’s fuller account.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the man. Barnes and Clarke: a centurion was “a commander of a hundred men” in the Roman armies, garrisoned in conquered Judea, “probably by birth a pagan.” The chief harmonizing question is that Matthew says the centurion “came” while Luke says he “sent” elders and friends. Calvin resolves it: there is “no impropriety in Matthew saying that the centurion did what was done in his name and at his request” — “it is absurd to make two miracles instead of one.” Wesley and Augustine agree, the Hebrew idiom ascribing “to a person himself the thing which is done… by his order” (as with Zebedee’s children, Matthew 20:20).

The fathers read the centurion as the firstfruits of the Gentiles. Pseudo-Chrysostom: the move from the mount to Capernaum is “a mystery, signifying that after the purification of the Jews he went to the Gentiles.” Augustine: “this centurion was of the Gentiles”; by declaring himself unworthy “he showed himself worthy, not… into whose house, but into whose heart, Christ… should enter.” Remigius notes the “hundred is a perfect number,” so the centurion figures “those of the Gentiles who first believed, and were perfect in virtue,” praying for the salvation of the whole Gentile world. Hilary reads the servant as “the head of the Gentiles that should believe.” The Puritan Henry marks the soldier’s godliness — “a little piety commonly goes a great way with men of that profession” — God having “his remnant” even in a garrison. Chrysologus reads the centurion’s house mystically as “the body which contained his soul,” into which God disdains not to enter; and Pseudo-Origen applies it to the holy men through whom “the Lord also enters” under our roof, and to the believer at the Lord’s Table. Hilary takes the servant as “the head of the Gentiles that should believe,” whose salvation, being “of faith,” needs only Christ’s word.
6:
On Matthew 8:6 — “Lord, my servant lieth at home sick of the palsy, grievously tormented” — the comment is brief and chiefly grammatical-historical, fixing the relation, the disease, and the master’s tender concern.

On the word “servant” (pais), Gill notes it “more commonly signifies a son or child,” but Luke’s use of the word for “slave” (doulos) settles it as a servant — “dear unto him,” held “in great esteem.” Ellicott agrees the Greek “might mean either ‘servant’ or ‘boy,’” fixed by Luke as slave. On the disease, Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson describe a violent paralysis: “grievously tormented” (deinōs basanizomenos) points to “more acute suffering than is common,” perhaps “rheumatic fever, or tetanus, or the special kind of paralysis which benumbs the muscles only, and affects the nerves of sensation with sharp pain” (Ellicott; compare 1 Maccabees 9:55–56). Lightfoot glosses “lieth” as “laid forth,” the man so prostrate that “he is moved only by others.”

The witnesses commend the master’s heart. Clarke contrasts him with masters who, when servants fall sick, “have them removed to an infirmary… or sent home to friends” who care little; this centurion “does not put his sick servant from him.” Rabanus marks that he “recounts with grief” the servant’s state — sick, palsied, tormented — “to move the Lord to have mercy,” teaching that “all ought to feel for their servants, and to take thought for them.” The very pity that brought the case to Christ, Ellicott observes, was “itself” a mark of grace in the man. Chrysostom and Rabanus note that the centurion recounts the servant’s misery — sick, palsied, tormented — not to excuse failing to bring him (impossible in such torment) but rather, says Chrysostom, as “a mark of his great faith,” deeming “a word alone… enough to restore” so that “he deemed it superfluous to bring him.” The detail that the servant was “dear unto him” (Luke) shows, Gill adds, both a good servant and a good master, who “does not put his sick servant from him.” Lightfoot glosses “lieth” (“laid forth”) as expressing “the power and dominion of the disease” — the man so prostrate he “is moved only by others” — the same word used of Peter’s mother-in-law a few verses on.
7:
On Matthew 8:7 — “And Jesus saith unto him, I will come and heal him” — the witnesses note Christ’s ready, even forward, willingness, and several read the words with precision.

The Reformed Gill finds in the short, full answer not only Christ’s readiness — “how soon and easily he complied” with a prayer of faith — but “an absolute promise that he would heal him”; indeed it offers “more than what was asked,” for “his presence was not asked, and yet he offered it.” The eastern Chrysostom marks the singular condescension: “Jesus here does what he never did; he always follows the wish of the supplicant, but here he goes before it, and not only promises to heal… but to go to his house” — done “that we may learn the worthiness of the centurion.”

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the wording. Clarke renders “I am coming, and will heal him,” a saying “worthy of observation”: Christ healed the servant “without going to the house,” so the promise was that “while he is coming, ere he arrives, he will heal him” — literally fulfilled in v.13. Robertson glosses the verb “heal” (therapeusō, to serve, give medical attention, then cure), noting the centurion uses the more definite word for healing (iathēsetai) in v.8. Ellicott, following Luke, supposes that as Jesus set out with the elders, word ran ahead, prompting the centurion’s humble second message. The offer to come is itself, the witnesses agree, the occasion that draws out the centurion’s remarkable faith. Gill notes the answer is “short and full,” proposing “more than what was asked of him,” since “his presence was not asked, and yet he offered it” — a readiness that both honours the suppliant’s prayer of faith and prepares the way for the yet greater faith that will decline the visit. Robertson observes that Luke, “like a physician,” distinguishes the words for treating and curing, though the distinction is “not always observed.” Ellicott, following Luke, supposes the answer was carried back by the elders as Jesus set out, prompting the centurion’s humbler second message — so that the very promptness of Christ’s offer becomes the occasion of the man’s deeper self-abasement.
8:
On Matthew 8:8 — “Lord, I am not worthy that thou shouldest come under my roof: but speak the word only, and my servant shall be healed” — the witnesses unite in admiring the centurion’s humility and the faith that rose above any need of bodily presence.

On the humility, Barnes calls it “an expression of great humility… a conviction of the great dignity and power of the Saviour,” the feeling “appropriate when… every truly penitent sinner” comes to Christ (Luke 15:19). Ellicott notes Luke’s report has the friends begin “Trouble not thyself,” the sense of unworthiness implying “at once the consciousness of his own sins, and the recognition of the surpassing holiness… of the Teacher.” Jerome marks the centurion’s “thoughtfulness” in seeing “the Divinity hidden beneath the covering of body.”

On the faith, the grammatical-historical Clarke prefers the reading “speak by word or command,” the pattern of “living faith and genuine humility”: “Jesus can will away the palsy, and speak away the most grievous torments.” Ellicott marks this “the special proof of the speaker’s faith”: he had risen “above the thought of a magic influence, operating by touch or charm, to that of a delegated power depending only on the will of Him who possessed it.” The Reformed Calvin names two points — the centurion “sparing Christ by way of honoring him” as unworthy of a visit, and ascribing to him “such power as to believe that, by the mere expression of his will, and by a word, his servant may recover” — “astonishing humility in a man who belonged to a conquered and enslaved nation.” The Alexandrian Pseudo-Origen hears the Church’s own confession at the Lord’s Table, “Lord, I am not worthy,” in the centurion’s words. Clarke draws from them “the pattern of that living faith and genuine humility which ought always to accompany the prayer of a sinner,” marking the two degrees of humility — to acknowledge our need of mercy and our inability to help ourselves, and to “confess the freeness of his grace, and our own utter unworthiness.” Augustine sums it: by declaring himself unworthy of Christ under his roof, the centurion showed himself worthy into whose heart Christ should enter.
9:
On Matthew 8:9 — “For I am a man under authority, having soldiers under me: and I say to this man, Go, and he goeth…” — the witnesses agree the centurion reasons from his own experience of command to Christ’s sovereign power over disease.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the argument. Barnes: “I am subject to the commands of others, and know how to obey”; as the soldiers obey him — “I say to one, Go, and he goeth” — “so do diseases, storms, and seas obey” Christ; “if men obey me, who am an inferior officer, how much more shall diseases obey” the Lord. Robertson notes the “also” (“even I in my subordinate position have soldiers under me”), so that, “as a military man,” he had learned to expect “instant obedience,” and had faith in Christ’s power “even without coming.” Clarke supplies the detail of the Roman ranks to clarify the centurion’s subordinate yet commanding place. Ellicott catches the “naïveté” of the reasoning: experience taught that “a delegated authority could, in its turn, be delegated to others,” so in God’s kingdom too “the unknown forces… that govern life and death” would “execute his will.”

The Reformed Calvin clarifies the logic: “this comparison does not imply equality… but is taken from the less to the greater,” the centurion forming “a higher conception of the divine power… than of the authority which he possessed over servants and soldiers.” Wesley: “I am only an inferior officer, and what I command is done even in my absence; how much more what thou commandest, who art Lord of all!” The Gloss spiritualizes the soldiers as the powers and angels at Christ’s command, who need only say to the disease “Go,” and it departs. The centurion’s faith, all agree, grasped that Christ’s bare word carried the authority of God over the whole realm of nature and sickness. Barnes spells out the reasoning: “as these obey me, so do diseases, storms, and seas obey you”; if soldiers obey an officer who is himself under command, how much more will sickness obey the Lord of all. Ellicott marvels that a Gentile soldier should so “reason on this analogy,” inferring that in God’s kingdom too the unseen powers stand ready to execute the King’s word — a faith that needed neither presence nor touch, but only the command of him whom all things serve.
10:
On Matthew 8:10 — “When Jesus heard it, he marvelled, and said… I have not found so great faith, no, not in Israel” — the witnesses ponder how Christ can “marvel,” and why a Gentile’s faith should be praised above Israel’s.

On the wonder, the Latin and Reformed voices agree it belongs to Christ as man. Augustine: “who was he that had created this faith… but only he who now marvelled at it?” — his wonder is not surprise in the all-knowing God but “to teach us what we ought to wonder at,” the emotions in him being “not signs of passion, but examples of a teacher.” Calvin: “wonder cannot apply to God… but it might exist in Christ, for he had clothed himself with our flesh, and with human affections.” Pseudo-Origen marvels at what God marvels at — not “gold, riches, principalities… but faith only.”

On the comparison, the grammatical-historical commentators note the faith’s peculiar quality. Ellicott: Israelites “craved for presence, or touch… here was one who believed in the power of the word.” Robertson: “greater than in any of the Jews,” as later with the Canaanite woman (Matthew 15:28). Barnes thinks him “the first Gentile convert,” an early earnest that “the heathen were to be brought to the truth”; Clarke, from Luke, makes him “a devout Gentile, a proselyte of the gate” who “loved our nation, and built us a synagogue” (Luke 7:5; compare Acts 10:1). Calvin qualifies that “the faith of Mary was greater” absolutely (Luke 1:38); the centurion’s is praised “in a particular point of view” — faith without the props the Jews demanded, found where none was expected. Chrysostom marks that Christ “marvelled in the presence of all the people, giving them an example that they also should wonder,” and that he “commended” rather than blamed the centurion’s testimony to his power. Pseudo-Chrysostom adds that even the apostles came to faith by degrees — Andrew by the Baptist’s word, Peter by the Scriptures, Nathanael by a proof — so that a Gentile believing on the bare word was indeed something rare. (Stray Cyril of Jerusalem material on eternal life is attached here.)
11:
On Matthew 8:11 — “many shall come from the east and west, and shall sit down with Abraham, and Isaac, and Jacob, in the kingdom of heaven” — the witnesses read in the centurion’s faith a foretaste of the call of the Gentiles.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the universal scope. Barnes: “from the east and from the west” denotes “the whole world” (Isaiah 45:6), and “shall sit down” refers to the reclining posture at meals, the joys of heaven figured “under the similitude of a feast” (Luke 14:15). Robertson glosses “recline at table” (anaklithēsontai), noting that to picture the patriarchs seated “in modern style” is an anachronism. Clarke observes the rabbis themselves figured “the blessedness of the kingdom” as a banquet, the saying meant “to soften the unreasonable prejudices of the Jews” against the Gentiles. Ellicott marks that Luke omits the words here (giving them in Luke 13:28), an instance of Christ’s “verbal reproduction of what had been said before.”

The fathers and Reformed dwell on the patriarchs and the harvest. Augustine notes Christ says “not ‘all,’ but many; yet these from the east and west,” the two quarters intending “the whole world”; and that Abraham sits in the kingdom because “the God of Abraham… is the Father of Christ” (Jerome). Calvin: in the servant Christ gave the Gentiles “a taste… of his grace,” and now shows the master “an example of the future calling of the Gentiles… throughout the whole world” — a thing “strange and incredible to the Jews, who imagined that God was confined to the family of Abraham.” Wesley: those from “the farthest parts of the earth shall… enjoy the rewards of the Gospel covenant established with Abraham.” Augustine reads “from the east” of those who pass into the kingdom “as soon as they are enlightened,” and “from the west” of those who come through suffering — the two quarters intending the whole world. Gill marks that Christ here makes “a short digression concerning the call of the Gentiles,” suggesting that what was seen in this one man “would be fulfilled in great numbers” gathered from “the four corners of the earth.” (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown note attached here strays to the mote and beam.)
12:
On Matthew 8:12 — “but the children of the kingdom shall be cast out into outer darkness: there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth” — the witnesses agree the “children” are the Jews, natural heirs who forfeit their inheritance, and the “darkness” a figure of judgment.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the Hebraism. Robertson: “the sons of the kingdom” is an idiom like “son of hell” (Matthew 23:15); the Jews felt “a natural right to the privileges of the kingdom because of descent from Abraham” (Matthew 3:9), but “mere natural birth did not bring spiritual sonship.” Barnes: they “supposed themselves peculiarly the favourites of heaven,” yet many would be lost and many pagans saved. On “outer darkness,” Ellicott, Clarke, and Robertson note the image continues the feast: the article (“the outer darkness,” “the weeping”) sets the dark outside against the king’s palace “blazing with lamps and torches,” the nuptial feasts being held at night.

The fathers and Reformed weigh the forfeiture. Calvin asks why they are called children who were “nothing less than children of Abraham,” and answers that, occupying “a place in the Church,” they are so designated, the covenant having “such force” that the heavenly inheritance “belonged peculiarly to them” until their rejection (Romans 11:16). Remigius reads a historical prediction of the Jews’ dispersion, assigning weeping (from heat) and gnashing (from cold) to the warmer and colder climes of their scattering. The dreadful reversal, on every reading, is that birthright without faith is forfeited, while believing strangers take the heirs’ place at the feast. Barnes notes the Jews “called themselves the children of the kingdom, to the exclusion of the Gentiles,” expecting the Messiah to enlarge their nation; Christ used “the manner of speech to which they were accustomed” to overturn the very confidence they rested in. Clarke adds that the “outer darkness” answers to the night outside the lighted banquet-house, the excluded shut out from both the light and the joy of the feast. Robertson notes the repeated article (“the darkness the outside”) makes the phrase “bolder and more impressive,” one of the standing figures for the place of punishment (Matthew 25:30).
13:
On Matthew 8:13 — “Go thy way; and as thou hast believed, so be it done unto thee. And his servant was healed in the selfsame hour” — the witnesses note the measure of faith and the completeness of the cure.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the proportion. Clarke: “let the mercy thou requestest be equal to the faith thou hast brought” — “according to thy faith be it done unto thee” is “a general measure of God’s dealings with mankind”; to increase faith is to increase “every grace.” Ellicott prefers “as thou didst believe,” referring to “his one great act of faith.” Barnes marks the completeness — “no miracle could be more complete… no imposition or deception” — and discusses whether this account and Luke’s are one or two events, inclining to one. Robertson and others observe the healing “in the selfsame hour” literally fulfilled the promise of v.7 to heal even before arriving.

The Reformed Calvin draws the doctrine: “how graciously Christ pours out his grace, when he finds the vessel of faith open.” Though spoken to the centurion, “in his person he invites us all to strong hope”; and we are taught why God is often “so limited in his communications to us — it is because our unbelief does not permit him to be liberal.” Gill notes Christ, the digression on the Gentiles ended, “returns an answer to the centurion agreeably to his desire.” The cure thus seals the lesson of the whole episode: the word of Christ, met by faith, accomplishes at a distance and at once what no presence or touch could do more surely. Adam Clarke draws the abiding rule: “to get an increase of faith is to get an increase of every grace,” God being “the same in the present time which he was in ancient days.” Barnes notes the completeness left “no imposition, or deception” possible, and that this account or one like it recurs in Luke 7:1–10, whether the same or a second centurion encouraged by the first. Ellicott prefers “as thou didst believe,” the aorist pointing to that “one great act of faith” which Christ now honours with the answering deed.
14:
On Matthew 8:14 — “Jesus came into Peter’s house, and saw his wife’s mother laid, and sick of a fever” — the witnesses note Peter’s marriage, his reverence, and the allegorical readings of the fathers.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the household. Barnes: “the mention of Peter’s wife’s mother proves that Peter either was or had been married” (1 Corinthians 9:5); Clarke cites Theophylact that “marriage is no hindrance to virtue, since the chief of the apostles had his wife,” and Ellicott notes the fact “has not unnaturally been… prominent in the Protestant argument against the enforced celibacy of the clergy.” Robertson glosses the two participles — bedridden (beblēmenēn) and burning with fever — a sudden, severe attack (Mark 1:30). Ellicott and others note from Mark and Luke it was the Sabbath, after teaching and a healing in the synagogue.

The eastern Chrysostom marks Peter’s reverence: though his mother-in-law lay sick, “he did not force him thither at once, but waited till his teaching should be completed”; Christ “went in of himself,” to show, after telling the centurion “I am not worthy that thou shouldest come under my roof,” what honour he does his own. The Latin fathers allegorize: Bede reads Peter’s house as the Law or circumcision, the mother-in-law the synagogue, sick “of zealous hate”; Remigius, the Law “made weak through the flesh,” healed when Christ “fulfilled the Law in action”; Hilary, the “sickly condition of infidelity”; Rabanus, the soul feverish with “fleshly lusts” that, touched by mercy, “restrains concupiscence by the bridle of continence.” Augustine raises again the differing order of the Evangelists, concluding it matters little “in what order the events are told… whether something omitted before is” later supplied. Gill notes the house was shared with Andrew, and that James and John went in too (Mark 1:29); Henry records it as an instance of Christ’s “kindness to the families” of his disciples, the fever being “nothing extraordinary” yet healed because the patient was Peter’s near relation. The mention of the mother-in-law, Barnes adds, “proves that Peter either was or had been married” (1 Corinthians 9:5), a fact much pressed against enforced celibacy.
15:
On Matthew 8:15 — “he touched her hand, and the fever left her: and she arose, and ministered unto them” — the comment is brief and chiefly grammatical-historical, marking the manner of healing and the completeness shown by her service.

On the touch, Gill notes the variety of Christ’s methods — “sometimes he healed by a word, as the centurion’s servant; and sometimes by a touch, as here; and sometimes by both, as the leper” — and that Luke adds he “stood over her” and “rebuked the fever,” having “all diseases, as well as the elements, at his beck.” Robertson reads the touch “in loving sympathy as the Great Physician,” and the ministering as a conative imperfect, “began to minister… at table in gratitude and love.” Clarke marvels: “can anything on this side the unlimited power of God effect such a cure with only a touch?” — and notes the strong manuscript reading “ministered to him,” drawing the lesson that “serving Christ in his ordinances and in his members is the best proof… of being soundly restored to spiritual health.”

On the completeness, Ellicott and the Gloss observe that the cure left none of the fever’s “usual sequel of weakness and exhaustion”: “it is not enough that she is cured, but strength is given her besides,” so that she rose at once and served. Jerome had already noted that whereas “naturally the greatest weakness follows fever… that health which is given by the Lord’s power is complete at once” — the instantaneous, total restoration being itself a token of divine power, and her immediate service the fitting fruit of a healing wholly received. Gill notes Christ healed variously — by word, by touch, by both — having “all diseases, as well as the elements, at his beck and control,” Luke adding that he “stood over her” and “rebuked the fever.” Clarke draws the lesson of the strong reading “ministered to him”: that “serving Christ in his ordinances and in his members is the best proof we can give… of being soundly restored to spiritual health” — the healed life turning at once to grateful service.
16:
On Matthew 8:16 — “when the even was come, they brought unto him many that were possessed with devils: and he cast out the spirits with a word, and healed all that were sick” — the witnesses note the timing, the sweep of the miracles, and the word that did them.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain “when the even was come.” Lightfoot, Clarke, and Ellicott observe it was the Sabbath (“the sun was now set,” Mark), so the people, who by their tradition “would… have been deterred” from carrying the sick on the Sabbath, waited until sunset closed it, when “they would feel themselves free to act” — the cool of evening serving too. Clarke and Lightfoot also explain why Judea then “abounded with demoniacs.” Robertson paints “a beautiful sunset scene” as “all the city gathered” at Peter’s door and Jesus healed them “with a word” — “a never to be forgotten memory.” Barnes marks the proof of “his great benevolence… and of his Divine mission,” none of his miracles wrought for mere display.

The eastern Chrysostom notes the Evangelist “runs through” a great multitude of cures “in one word,” introducing “an innumerable flood of miracles,” and brings forward the prophet (Isaiah) to forestall unbelief that so many could be healed so quickly. Remigius: “daemons and diseases he sent away with a word, that… he might show that he was come for the salvation of the human race.” The sunset, Rabanus adds, “shadows forth the passion,” after which the light promised to all the Gentiles — the evening healings thus pointing, for the fathers, beyond the day’s mercies to the saving work they prefigure. Barnes marks the double proof in the indiscriminate healing of “all that were sick”: of “his great benevolence” and of “his Divine mission,” none of his miracles wrought “merely to make a display of power.” Hilary adds that “by the passion of his body… he absorbed all the infirmities of human weakness,” the prophet’s word (quoted next) being the key to the whole evening’s work. Robertson paints the unforgettable scene as “all the city gathered” at Peter’s door at sundown, and Jesus, standing in the doorway, healed them every one “with a word.”
17:
On Matthew 8:17 — “that it might be fulfilled which was spoken by Esaias… Himself took our infirmities, and bare our sicknesses” — the witnesses agree the quotation is from Isaiah 53:4, and discuss how a prophecy of atonement is applied to bodily healing.

The grammatical-historical commentators address the application. Barnes notes the Hebrew of Isaiah “fully states the doctrine of atonement,” that the Messiah was to “suffer for sin,” the word meaning “diseases of the body” here used of the very truth Matthew declares. Robertson surveys the renderings — “he took away our sicknesses, and bore the burden of our diseases” — noting Matthew makes “a free interpretation of the Hebrew.” Ellicott calls the citation “interesting as showing Matthew’s way of dealing with Messianic prophecies”: the chapter throughout pictures “the spiritual work of redemption,” yet the Evangelist, “with the memory of that evening present to his mind, saw them fulfilled” in the removal of bodily infirmities.

The Reformed Calvin resolves the apparent strain: Isaiah speaks “of the death of Christ… of spiritual and eternal grace,” while Matthew applies it to bodily diseases — but the Evangelist states “not merely the benefit… but the purpose for which he healed,” for they “experienced in their bodies the grace of Christ,” a pledge of the deeper deliverance. Wesley: Isaiah “spoke it in a more exalted sense”; Christ fulfilled it “in the highest sense, by bearing our sins in his own body on the tree… in a lower sense, by sympathizing with us… and healing us of the diseases which were the fruit of sin” — such double fulfilment being “elegancies rather than imperfections” in Scripture. Rabanus reads “took” not as keeping but as taking away from us what “we were too weak to bear” — he “bare our sicknesses, in that what we were too weak to bear, he should bear for us.” Lightfoot adds the striking rabbinic tradition that one name of the Messiah was “the leper… one that carries infirmities,” the Jews themselves grounding it on this very verse of Isaiah. Robertson surveys the renderings — “he took away our sicknesses, and bore the burden of our diseases” — and notes the difficulty of fixing “in what sense Matthew applies the words,” whether in the precise Hebrew sense or freely. (Stray Cyril and JFB fragments are attached here.)
18:
On Matthew 8:18 — “when Jesus saw great multitudes about him, he gave commandment to depart unto the other side” — the witnesses read the crossing as a withdrawal for humility and rest, and note the geography.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the place and the motive. Barnes and Clarke: Jesus was at Capernaum “at the north-west corner of the sea of Tiberias,” purposing to cross to the eastern shore, “the country of the Gergesenes” (v.28). Ellicott marks, “though less conspicuously than in the other Gospels,” the “yearning for a time of rest and retirement,” and Wesley: he commanded the crossing “that both himself and the people might have a little rest.” Calvin notes Matthew “touches briefly what the others explain… more copiously,” and that “the other side” need not mean the strictly opposite shore but “that curvature of the lake which was below Capernaum.”

The eastern Chrysostom reads the command as a lesson in lowliness: Christ “not only healed the body, but… desired to show true wisdom… by doing nothing with ostentation,” and bade his disciples cross “that he might not offend” the crowds — not dismissing them, so “the crowds might hope to be able to follow.” Remigius adds that they “hung upon him in admiration… for who would depart from one who did such miracles?” The Puritan Henry draws the missionary note: “the influences of this Sun of righteousness were not to be confined to one place… the necessities of souls called to him, Come over, and help us” (Acts 16:9). The crossing thus turns the narrative from the crowds of Capernaum toward the further shore and the works that await there. Chrysostom notes Christ “does not dismiss the multitudes, that he may not offend them,” bidding only the disciples cross, “thus the crowds might hope to be able to follow.” Calvin observes that Matthew “touches briefly what the others explain… more copiously,” Mark and Luke noting that Christ withdrew privately for retirement; the crossing is thus both a seeking of rest and a widening of his work, that the “influences of this Sun of righteousness” might not be “confined to one place” (Henry).
19:
On Matthew 8:19 — “a certain scribe came, and said unto him, Master, I will follow thee whithersoever thou goest” — the witnesses read the offer as over-eager and self-confident, and note the striking fact that it came from a scribe.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the man and his motive. Barnes thinks he “had formed an expectation that by following Jesus he would obtain some considerable worldly advantage,” a hope Christ at once dashed. Robertson calls him “a bumptious brother full of self-confidence,” already a disciple (since v.21 speaks of “another of the disciples”), yet “of that most unimpressionable class… utterly opposed to the ways of Jesus,” whom “Jesus deals gently with.” Ellicott marks it “striking, as showing that the impression made by our Lord’s teaching was not confined to the common people”: as Nicodemus had owned him “a Teacher come from God,” so here a scribe, moved by the Sermon, volunteers “at least the show of discipleship.” Clarke notes the single scribe shows “how few of this class came to the Lord Jesus.”

The Reformed Calvin sets the two would-be disciples (three in Luke) side by side: it seems strange that Christ “sends back one who offers to follow him immediately” yet “detains another” who asked delay, but “there are the best reasons for both.” The scribe’s “great readiness” sprang from imagining “that he would enjoy an easy life” — a presumption Christ must correct before any true following can begin. The verse thus opens a pair of cautionary cases: the cost of discipleship must be counted, and counted truly. Clarke notes the scribe addresses Christ as “Master” (teacher), and that “a man not illuminated by the Spirit of God thinks himself capable of any thing” — offering to follow “whithersoever” before he has weighed where that may lead. Gill places the scene “as they went in the way” to the seaside; the offer, on every reading, is bold but untried, and Christ’s reply will test whether it springs from faith or from the hope of gain. Ellicott marks it “striking” that the volunteer was a scribe, showing the Sermon’s impression reached beyond “the common people that heard him gladly” even to one of the learned class.
20:
On Matthew 8:20 — “The foxes have holes, and the birds of the air have nests; but the Son of man hath not where to lay his head” — the witnesses agree Christ answers the scribe’s worldly hope with the homelessness of his own life, and note the first appearance of the title “Son of man.”

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the poverty. Barnes: “the very foxes and birds have places of repose… but the Son of man has no home, and no pillow… a stranger in his own world” (John 1:11). Ellicott: to follow him “was not to be the adherent of a new sect… or the servant of a king marching to an earthly throne, but to share in poverty, privation, homelessness” — the scribe, like the rich young ruler, “had not counted the cost.” Robertson glosses “holes” (burrows) and “nests” (roosts, leafy shelters), and notes this is the first of the many self-designations “Son of man.” Clarke and Wesley trace the title to Daniel 7:13 and Psalm 8:4, “expressive of humiliation,” chosen “out of humility” for his “mean appearance in this world.”

The Reformed Calvin reads it as a warning to the scribe “not to expect an abundant and rich hire, as if he had a wealthy master, while the master himself receives a precarious subsistence in borrowed houses”; it describes “what was Christ’s condition… and informs his disciples what sort of life they must be prepared to expect.” Clarke draws the comfort for the poor and godly: “what comfort to derive from the thought that thou so nearly resemblest the Lord Jesus,” over against the rich “slave of sin.” The answer, on every reading, neither accepts nor refuses the scribe outright but lays before him the true terms of following a homeless Lord. Robertson notes the title appears here “for the first time” and that Jesus “deliberately” chose it, full of meaning “for the Speaker.” Gill marks the dignity beneath the lowliness: though without a place to lay his head, he is the same Son of man “to whom is given dominion, and glory, and a kingdom” (Daniel 7:14). The homelessness named is real, yet voluntary — the Lord of all choosing privation, so that whoever follows must follow not for ease but for himself.
21:
On Matthew 8:21 — “Lord, suffer me first to go and bury my father” — the witnesses read this second man as one already a disciple, whose fault was not over-eagerness but over-caution, a lawful tie put before the immediate call.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the request. Barnes: “disciple” here means a learner, “one of the multitude,” not necessarily “a pious man” (John 6:66). Clarke notes the Jewish maxim that a duty to the dead freed a man for the time “from the observance of any other precept,” and that “the children of Adam are always in extremes” — some rushing into ministry uncalled, others delaying after they are called. Robertson and Ellicott observe it is “by no means certain that the father was dead”: the words may mean “after my father is dead and buried, I will follow” (compare Tobit’s charge, “Son, when I am dead, bury me,” Tobit 4:3), an Oriental still being known to say, with his father at his side, “I must first bury my father.” Ellicott reports the tradition (from Clement) that this disciple was Philip, though “nothing in the Gospel history suggests this.”

The Reformed Calvin contrasts him with the scribe: the one was rejected for rash presumption, this one “retained” despite his fault — “prevented from immediately obeying… by the weakness of thinking it a hardship to leave his father,” probably aged. He “does not refuse the calling: he only asks leave for a time” to discharge a filial duty. Hilary reads the request as showing the man already believed he ought to follow, the question being only of delay. The verse sets the second snare of discipleship: a good and natural duty allowed to postpone the higher claim of Christ. Clarke warns that “the children of Adam are always in extremes—some will rush into the ministry… without a call, others will delay long after they are called,” the “middle way” alone being safe. Robertson reads the request as overcaution to set against the scribe’s overconfidence: “the first man was an enthusiast; this one is overcautious,” yet “Jesus wanted first things first.” Whether the father lay already dead or merely aged, the principle holds: service to Christ takes precedence even over a sacred filial duty.
22:
On Matthew 8:22 — “Follow me; and let the dead bury their dead” — the witnesses agree the saying turns on two senses of “dead,” and that Christ asserts the priority of his call over even sacred duties, without condemning burial.

The grammatical-historical commentators unfold the paradox. Barnes: the word “dead” is used “in two different senses” — the Jews used it to express indifference or want of influence (to be “dead to the law,” Romans 7:4; “dead to sin,” Romans 6:11) — so “let the spiritually dead bury the naturally dead.” Clarke: “leave the spiritually dead to bury their natural dead… to preach the glad tidings… is granted but to a few.” Robertson: “the spiritually dead are always on hand to bury the physically dead, if one’s real duty is with Jesus,” adding Chrysostom’s word that “while it is a good deed to bury the dead, it is a better one to preach Christ.” Ellicott reads the half-proverbial saying as bidding those “with no spiritual life” keep the “outward routine duties,” while the truly living do their Master’s work.

The Reformed Calvin guards the saying: “Christ does not condemn burial,” which “originated in a divine command” and strengthens “the hope of the last resurrection”; he means only that “whatever withdraws us from the right course… deserves no other name” than death. Those alone truly live who “devote all their thoughts… to obedience to God.” Wesley sums it: “when God calls, leave the business of the world to them who are dead to God.” The pair of cases together teach, on every reading, that the call of Christ overrides both the lure of gain and the pull of even legitimate earthly ties. Clarke notes the Jewish usage that reckoned “every transgressor” a dead man, so the saying “had nothing difficult in it to a Jew”; Ellicott infers there was “something in the inward state of the disciple which called for the sternness of the rebuke.” Calvin adds that those alone truly live “who devote all their thoughts… to obedience to God,” while those who “do not rise above the world” are, for all their activity, reckoned among the dead.
23:
On Matthew 8:23 — “when he was entered into a ship, his disciples followed him” — the witnesses set the scene of the stilling of the storm, and the fathers read the boat and voyage allegorically.

The grammatical-historical commentators fix the setting. Ellicott notes the better text reads “the ship” — one “belonging possibly to Peter or the sons of Zebedee, always ready at their Master’s service” — and that Mark’s “they took him, even as he was” indicates “extreme exhaustion from the fatigue of teaching,” followed by immediate sleep “on the boat’s cushion.” Barnes describes “a small open boat with sails,” other ships in company (Mark 4:36) making the miracle “more striking.” John Chrysostom notes Matthew “maintains the order” here while Luke and Mark do not, and that Christ took the disciples “to make them spectators of the miracle.”

The fathers read the voyage spiritually. Pseudo-Origen: the disciples followed “not being weak but strong… in the faith,” accompanying him “in holiness of spirit.” Chrysostom: he took only the disciples and let them be “tossed by the waves,” that they “not think great things of themselves” — “where temptations and fears were to be stilled, there he takes with him only the victors… whom he would prepare for strife.” Rabanus reads the sea as “the turmoil of the world” and the boat as “the tree of the cross,” by which the faithful cross to their “heavenly country”; Bede, “the present Church, in which Christ passes over the sea of this world… and stills the waves of persecution.” The Puritan Henry notes Christ “chose to go by water… that he might… manifest himself the God of the sea as well as of the dry land,” crossing to “rescue a poor creature” on the far shore though he foresaw the affront he would meet. Pseudo-Origen observes that, having entered the boat, Christ “caused the sea to rise,” the tempest arising not “of itself, but in obedience” to his purpose of testing and training the disciples. Chrysostom adds that he let them be “tossed by the waves” lest they “think great things of themselves” because he had kept them with him while sending the rest away.
24:
On Matthew 8:24 — “there arose a great tempest in the sea… but he was asleep” — the witnesses describe the sudden violence of the storm and the calm of the sleeping Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the squall. Robertson: the Sea of Galilee lies “680 feet below the Mediterranean,” and “these sudden squalls come down from the summit of Hermon with terrific force,” Mark and Luke calling it a whirlwind (lailaps). Ellicott: “the sea, which a few minutes before was smooth as glass, is at once rough”; the ship “on the point of sinking… and still he slept.” Barnes notes he slept “on the hinder part of the vessel, on a pillow” (Mark 4:38), showing “how calmly one can sleep with a pure conscience… safe in the hands of God.”

On the storm’s cause, the Reformed Gill says it arose “not by chance, nor by the power of Satan, but by divine providence, for the trial of the faith” of the disciples, while Clarke supposes it “excited by Satan, the prince of the power of the air,” who, having “the author and all the preachers of the Gospel together in a small vessel, thought by drowning it to defeat the purposes of God.” Jerome finds “a type in Jonah, who while all are in danger is himself unconcerned, sleeps, and is awakened,” and draws the lesson that “all creation is conscious of its Creator.” Wesley adds the proverb: “man’s extremity is God’s opportunity.” The sleeping Christ, the witnesses agree, both proves his true humanity and sets the stage for the display of his divine power. Gill notes the tempest was “a great concussion” that shook both ship and men, Luke and Mark using a word for “a whirlwind, or hurricane.” Hilary reads the sleep allegorically — “by our sloth he is cast asleep in us” — so that the storm of fear should drive us to hope “aid from God… by the might of Christ watching within.” The contrast of the raging sea and the calm sleeper, Ellicott observes, only heightens the wonder of the word that will still it.
25:
On Matthew 8:25 — “his disciples came to him, and awoke him, saying, Lord, save us: we perish” — the witnesses hear in the cry both real faith and a panic that Christ will rebuke.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the mingling of trust and terror. Barnes: the cry “showed great confidence in the Saviour… where sinners and Christians should always go who feel they are in danger of perishing” — “there is none that can save from the storms of Divine wrath but the Son of God.” Robertson renders it sharply: “Lord, save us at once (aorist), we are perishing (present linear).” Ellicott notes Mark’s harsher form, “Master, carest thou not that we perish?” betraying “the impatience of panic” — they began to think him “indifferent to their safety.” Clarke draws the use of trials: “to make us know our weakness, so as to oblige us to have recourse” to Christ.

The Reformed Calvin probes wherein they sinned, since the prayer seems “good and holy”: “they attached too much importance to the bodily presence of their Master,” expostulating rather than simply trusting; “they ought to have believed that the Divinity of Christ was not oppressed by carnal sleep, and to his Divinity they ought to have had recourse,” instead of doing nothing “till they were overcome by fear.” The cry thus stands as the church’s own in every storm — faith enough to call on Christ, yet faith too small to rest until it hears his voice. Clarke draws the use of trials, “to make us know our weakness, so as to oblige us to have recourse” to the Saviour; Barnes adds that the cry shows “where sinners and Christians should always go who feel that they are in danger of perishing,” since “there is none that can save from the storms of Divine wrath but the Son of God.” Their very alarm, the witnesses agree, became the occasion of a deeper lesson in the sufficiency of Christ. Robertson renders the urgency exactly — “Lord, save us at once… we are perishing” — the aorist of sudden appeal meeting the present tense of a danger already upon them.
26:
On Matthew 8:26 — “Why are ye fearful, O ye of little faith? Then he arose, and rebuked the winds and the sea; and there was a great calm” — the witnesses note the gentle reproof, the word of command, and the instant calm.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the rebuke and the miracle. Clarke: “faith is ever bold — incredulity always timid”; “Lord, increase our faith! is a necessary prayer.” Ellicott notes Luke’s sharper “Where is your faith?” yet finds “of little faith” apt: they had not lost their trust but “were only at ease when they heard his voice.” He marks that to rebuke the winds “seems… our Lord’s formula in working miracles” — the fever (Luke 4:39), the frenzy of the demoniac, the tempest, all “treated as if they were hostile” powers. Barnes calls it “irresistible proof that he was Divine… his word awed the tempest” — “there is not anywhere a sublimer description of a display of power.”

The Reformed Calvin draws out Mark’s word to the sea, “Peace, be still”: not that the lake had perception, but to show “the power of his voice reached the elements,” even as God “maketh wars to cease” (Psalm 46:9). Wesley marks the order: “first he composed their spirits, and then the sea.” Clarke notes that “the agitation of the sea was only the effect of the wind,” so Christ “removed the cause” that the effect might cease. The reproof and the calm together rebuke the disciples’ fear and reveal the Lord whom wind and wave obey. Gill notes that though they had “some faith in him, yet there was a great deal of fear and unbelief,” for which Christ gently blames them. Clarke draws the parallel with Joshua’s command to the sun: as Christ bade the sea “be silent,” restraining the wind that was “the proper cause of the disturbance,” the effect at once ceased — the elements owning a voice that man’s rebellion alone resists. Ellicott notes that to “rebuke” the winds was, as it were, Christ’s “formula in working miracles,” the tempest, the fever, and the demoniac’s frenzy all addressed “as if they were hostile” powers to be put down. (A stray Cyril of Jerusalem fragment on the sign of Jonah is attached here.)
27:
On Matthew 8:27 — “the men marvelled, saying, What manner of man is this, that even the winds and the sea obey him!” — the witnesses agree the miracle revealed Christ’s divine glory, and discuss who the wondering “men” were.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the proof of deity. Barnes: “they felt that he must be God himself… none but God could calm the heaving billows.” Robertson calls it “a nature miracle,” dismissing the rationalist evasion that “by an astonishing coincidence the storm happened to lull” — “some minds are easily satisfied by their own stupidities,” since “even a sudden drop in the wind would not at once calm the sea.” Clarke: “every part of the creation (man excepted) hears and obeys the Creator’s voice… here was God fully manifest, but it was in the flesh.” Ellicott notes the vague term “men” for the disciples “is so exceptional as to suggest… there were others in the boat,” and that the marvel was joined to “a great fear” (Mark 4:41).

The Reformed Calvin weighs whether the words are the apostles’ or others’: more probably “these words were spoken by others” not yet knowing Christ, the miracle making “such an impression… as to prove” his glory. The Gloss preserves Chrysostom’s neat antithesis: “his sleeping and his appearance showed the man; the sea and the calm pointed out the God.” Ellicott adds the spiritual application, now “almost a commonplace,” yet of “profound fitness”: the boat is the Church, tossed yet safe because Christ, though he seem to sleep, is aboard and is Lord of the storm. Clarke draws the contrast of the obedient creation and rebellious man: “every part of the creation (man excepted) hears and obeys the Creator’s voice.” Calvin notes the result of the miracle “appears in the display of the glory of Christ,” whether the words be the apostles’ or others’; and Barnes finds “no scene… more grand than this display of the power of Jesus” — the darkness, the waves, the howling winds, and then the sudden, awed stillness. (A second stray Cyril fragment on the sign of Jonah is attached here.)
28:
On Matthew 8:28 — “when he was come to the other side… there met him two possessed with devils, coming out of the tombs, exceeding fierce” — the witnesses address the place-name, the number of demoniacs, and the meaning of the tombs.

The grammatical-historical commentators reconcile the readings. Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, and Robertson note the variants — Gadarenes, Gerasenes, Gergesenes — and resolve them by geography: Gadara was a chief city, Gerasa (the modern Khersa, by the lake) a town within its district, so the region could be named from either, “no contradiction” among the Evangelists. On the number, Matthew has two, Mark and Luke one; all agree (with Augustine, Calvin, Wesley) that the others “chose to speak of the one who was more grievously afflicted,” “a person of note” whose cure was “the more noised abroad” — no denial that there were two. Lightfoot draws from the rabbis “the signs of a madman”: he “goes out in the night, lodges among the sepulchres, and teareth his garments.”

The fathers read the scene as the Gentile world. Rabanus interprets Gerasa as “casting out the dweller” — the Gentile world that “cast out the Devil… first far off, but now made near.” Ambrose and Hilary take the two demoniacs as a type of the Gentile nations (from Ham and Japheth), held by the demons “among the tombs without the town, that is, without the synagogue of the Law.” Chrysostom notes the demons “came to proclaim his divinity,” that those who had not seen the calmed sea “might hear the demons crying.” The Puritan Henry frames the chapter’s climax: Christ’s “dominion over devils, which to us are most formidable of all” (Ephesians 1:21) — he “has the keys of hell too,” principalities made subject to him even in his humiliation. Robertson describes the “tombs” as “chambers cut into the mountain side,” the limestone cliffs on the eastern shore “full of caves,” a fit and dreadful haunt for the possessed. Augustine adds that God was known to the demons “not as he is Life eternal… but by certain temporal effects of his excellence,” signs of his hidden presence that even evil spirits could not mistake.
29:
On Matthew 8:29 — “What have we to do with thee, Jesus, thou Son of God? art thou come hither to torment us before the time?” — the witnesses discuss the demons’ confession, the title they use, and their dread of premature torment.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the phrase. Barnes and Clarke render “what hast thou to do with us?” a Hebrew idiom of “abrupt refusal” or a wish not to be troubled (2 Samuel 16:10; 2 Kings 9:18). Robertson notes the demons treat Jesus as truly known — “the devil knew Jesus clearly” — and that they “know there is nothing in common between them and the Son of God,” fearing “torment before the time.” Ellicott observes Mark and Luke give “Son of the Most High God,” a title likely familiar “from the formulae of exorcism” (Acts 16:17).

The fathers weigh how far the demons knew him. Jerome: “no voluntary confession… but one extorted by the compulsion of necessity,” like a runaway slave dreading the scourge; the demons “rather suspected than knew” him the Son of God. Pseudo-Augustine, with 1 Corinthians 2:8: “had they known him, they never would have suffered the Lord of glory to be crucified.” Remigius notes they wavered — thinking him the Son of God when tortured by his power, doubting when they saw him hungry and suffering — and that their confession condemns the unbelieving Jews and the Arians alike. The Reformed Calvin: “the devils breathe nothing but rebellion against God; and yet… are crushed and fall in a moment,” compelled to yield though their malice is never subdued. Wesley glosses “before the time” as “the great day.” Gill marks it “an instance and proof of the wonderful power of Christ over the devils”: those who had made the men so fierce that “there was no passing the way” now “cry out… in great consternation… with the utmost subjection to him.” Robertson presses that the recognition is no folk-lore: “Jesus is represented as treating the demons as real existences separate from the human personality,” who “know there is nothing in common between them and the Son of God” and dread their appointed torment. (A stray Cyril of Jerusalem fragment on the witnesses to Christ is attached here.)
30:
On Matthew 8:30 — “there was a good way off from them a herd of many swine feeding” — the comment is brief and chiefly grammatical-historical, fixing the location and the puzzle of swine in Israel.

Gill harmonizes the distance — “a good way off” yet “within sight,” agreeing with Mark’s “nigh unto the mountains.” Barnes notes the herd numbered “two thousand” (Mark 5:13), and that “herd,” once given to any collection of beasts, is now reserved for cattle. The chief difficulty is the keeping of unclean animals. Lightfoot cites the rabbinic infamy and canon — “the wise men forbade to keep hogs anywhere” — both for the damage they did and as a thing accursed. Clarke and Wesley infer the swine were “in all probability Jewish property, kept… in express violation of the law of God,” so that their destruction was “a proper manifestation of the justice of God.”

Ellicott offers the alternative that, though the Jews ate no pork, “Roman soldiers did, and the swine may have been kept to supply… the legion,” recalling the pun of Augustus about Herod’s swine and son. Whether owned by transgressing Jews or by Gentiles supplying the garrison, the herd stands ready, the witnesses agree, both as the occasion of the demons’ request and as the means by which their malice and Christ’s power will presently be displayed before the whole country. The nearness of so large a herd to the tombs of the demoniacs sets the stage for the strange transaction that follows. Lightfoot piles up the rabbinic evidence of the infamy — “it was a matter of infamy for a Jew to keep swine,” and “the wise men forbade to keep hogs anywhere” — both for the damage they did and as accursed creatures. Whether the owners were transgressing Jews or Gentiles supplying the garrison, the witnesses agree the herd’s presence is itself a token of disorder, and its impending destruction will fall, by Christ’s leave, where the Law had already been set at nought. Gill notes the herd was “nigh unto the mountains” yet within sight, so that all might witness what befell it; the very number — two thousand — making the coming sign the more public and undeniable.
31:
On Matthew 8:31 — “the devils besought him, saying, If thou cast us out, suffer us to go away into the herd of swine” — the witnesses note the demons’ forced submission, their request, and its hidden aim.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the parallel reports. Ellicott: Mark has them beg “that he should not send them out of the country,” Luke “that he would not command them to go out into the deep” — the abyss, the “bottomless pit” (Revelation 9:1–2); the man’s words are the demons’, who shrink from wandering “in dry places, seeking rest and finding none” (Matthew 12:43). Clarke prefers the reading “send us away,” which “express[es] more fully the absolute power” of Christ — “the very power by which they were to go must come from Christ himself,” who could not be resisted even over the vilest animals (Matthew 4:9).

The Reformed Calvin probes the motive: not mere “enmity to all God’s creatures,” though that is true, but “a more remote object — to excite the inhabitants… to curse God on account of the loss of the swine,” as when Satan struck Job’s house “not from any hatred to timber or stones” but to provoke impatience against God (Job 1:11–12). Gill marks that the whole legion, denied leave to stay in the country, “begged hard” for the swine, and that the demons can do nothing — even to swine — “without divine permission.” Wesley notes Christ’s “Go” was “a word of permission only, not command.” The request itself betrays both the demons’ impotence and their unceasing design against God and man. Clarke marks how “vain was the boast of Satan” in the temptation (Matthew 4:9), “when we find he could not possess the body of one of the vilest animals… without the permission of Christ.” Ellicott notes the man’s words are wholly the demons’, “with whom he identifies himself,” shrinking from the abyss; the whole legion, Gill adds, “begged hard” not to be cast out of the country where they had long worked their mischief. Even their petition must be granted before they can stir.
32:
On Matthew 8:32 — “he said unto them, Go. And… the whole herd of swine ran violently down a steep place into the sea, and perished” — the witnesses agree the word was permission, and address why Christ allowed the loss.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the display of power and reject the rationalist evasion. Robertson: they “rushed down from the cliff into the sea” — here is “the mastery of the mind of the Master over the minds of the maniacs, the power of Christ over the demons,” the destruction of the hogs needing not trouble us. Ellicott rejects the notion “that the demoniacs drove the swine down in a last paroxysm of frenzy,” since “our Lord’s words sanctioned what they did,” the deliverance requiring some such sign to free the man from the “confusion between himself and the unclean spirits.” Clarke sees “an emblem of the final… ruin into which the swinish… impure” fall.

The Reformed Gill and Calvin justify the permission. Gill: God “only hears demons… when their prayer is the echo of his own justice”; if the owners were Jews keeping swine unlawfully, “it was a just punishment,” and if Gentiles, a means of conviction. Christ “suffered the devils to enter the herd… that he might give a proof” of the reality and malignity of the possession from which the men were freed, and of his own absolute power. The drowning of the swine, the witnesses agree, both manifested the saving deliverance of the men and unmasked the destructive intent of the spirits, while teaching that not even the lowest creature is touched but by the Lord’s leave. Robertson reflects on “the influence of mind on matter” and “the mastery of the mind of the Master,” plain enough “if we take Jesus to be really Lord and Saviour,” the incidental loss of the hogs no stranger than “nature’s tragedies which we cannot comprehend.” Gill adds that Christ suffered it “to give a proof” of the reality of the possession from which the men were freed — the visible fury of the drowning herd showing what the spirits had wrought in the men.
33:
On Matthew 8:33 — “they that kept them fled, and went their ways into the city, and told every thing, and what was befallen to the possessed of the devils” — the comment is brief, marking the keepers’ flight and their report.

The Reformed Gill describes the herdsmen as “men of very low life, whose employment was infamous”; “amazed at what they had seen, distressed with the loss of the swine… and frightened” lest they incur their masters’ displeasure, they “ran away in great haste, fear, and astonishment.” The grammatical-historical Barnes notes the swine were “doubtless owned by the inhabitants of Gadara,” and weighs again whether Jews (violating Leviticus 11:7) or Gentiles; the keepers “fled in consternation… amazed at his power,” carrying word both of the swine and of the healed demoniacs.

The Latin Bede reads the flight allegorically: that the keepers “fled and told what was done” signifies “that even the leaders of the wicked, though they shun the law of Christianity, yet cease not to proclaim the wonderful power of Christ.” Their report, told in fear rather than faith, carries the fame of the miracle into the city and so brings out the whole population — the report itself becoming, beyond the keepers’ intention, a testimony to the works of Christ. The terror of those who fed the swine thus serves to spread the news of both the judgment on the herd and the mercy shown the men. Barnes weighs again whether the keepers were Jews violating the law (Leviticus 11:7) or Gentiles, the region being a “mixture” on Judea’s border; either way they “fled in consternation… amazed at his power.” Their report carried into the city “every thing, and what was befallen to the possessed” — so that the very men whose trade was lost became the unwilling heralds of the miracle, drawing out the whole population to meet Christ. Bede draws the allegory once more: that the keepers “fled and told” signifies that “even the leaders of the wicked, though they shun the law of Christianity, yet cease not to proclaim the wonderful power of Christ” — the report of judgment serving, beyond their intent, to magnify him.
34:
On Matthew 8:34 — “the whole city came out to meet Jesus: and… they besought him that he would depart out of their coasts” — the witnesses agree the people preferred their swine to the Saviour, and read the request as the tragedy of avarice.

The grammatical-historical commentators expose the motive. Barnes: they came “probably with a view of arresting him for the injury done to the property,” but, “awed by his presence,” only begged him to leave — showing “the power of avarice… they loved their property so much that they were blind to… the miracle, and to the good he had done.” Robertson: “they cared more for hogs than for human souls, as often happens today.” Clarke: “many rather chose to lose Jesus Christ than those temporal goods… they love even their swine better than their salvation.” Wesley: “they loved their swine so much better than their souls! How many are of the same mind!”

Ellicott adds, from Mark and Luke, the contrasting picture of the healed demoniac “clothed, and in his right mind, sitting at the feet of Jesus,” the garment perhaps supplied “by the pity of the disciples” — and notes it “characteristic of the wild, half-heathen population” to see in the Prophet “a Destroyer rather than” a deliverer. Barnes draws the double lesson: “the design of Satan is to prejudice men against the Saviour,” and the “power of avarice” that prefers gain to grace. So the chapter of mighty works closes, paradoxically, with a city that, granted the clearest proof of divine power and mercy, asks its Author to go away — a sobering counterpoint to the centurion’s faith with which it began. Robertson notes they were “forgetful of the healing of the demoniacs in their concern over the loss of property.” Gill records that “the whole multitude of the country… round about” came, drawn by the news yet moved only to dread; Clarke draws the lesson that men “love even their swine better than their salvation,” preferring “temporal goods by which they gratify their passions” to the presence of the Saviour who would have enriched their souls.
